metadata.title: Why AI Can't Fix Its Own Mistakes

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Namaste, Liam — in for Bear. Why didn't self-review fix the error? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Why didn't self-review fix the error??
props.segment: Why AI Can't Fix Its Own

beats.narration_text: She found a reasoning error in Claude's analysis. She asked Claude to review its own reasoning and fix the error. The revision came back with a note: logic strengthened. The error was still there, now stated more confidently.

card.copy: Why AI can't fix its own mistakes
card.sub: the sieve that generates also reviews

beats.narration_text: Sofia is a policy consultant. She asks Claude to analyze whether a new intake process will reduce wait times. Claude produces a causal chain. She asks Claude to review it for errors. Claude revises and notes it strengthened the logic. Her director reads it and spots the flaw in sixty seconds.
beats.scene_description: Policy analyst at desk reading an analysis document, looking concerned, editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, policy analyst woman at a desk, looking concerned while reading a printed analysis document, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage

beats.narration_text: Sofia asked Claude to review and fix its own reasoning. Claude reviewed it, reported fixing issues, and produced a revised output. The original reasoning error remained. Why didn't self-review fix the error?

card.copy: Why didn't self-review fix the error?

beats.narration_text: A sieve holds what passes through its holes. A reasoning model is a sieve. Its holes are the gaps in its reasoning — the inference steps it cannot reliably make. An output that passes through the sieve contains the errors the sieve cannot catch.

When you ask Claude to review its own output, you are running the same sieve twice. The holes are in the same places. The errors that fell through the first time fall through again — because the weights that produced the error are the same weights doing the review.

What does come back from self-review? Surface-level improvements. Grammar, structure, word choice. These pass through the sieve because they are not in the holes. And the revision may even sound more confident — the model is now committed to the reasoning it already produced.

Self-critique is useful — but for a different purpose. It surfaces candidate issues for the human reviewer to inspect. It is not a mechanism for the model to repair its own logical gaps. Those require an external check.

card.copy: Self-critique = surfacing for you, not repairing for the model

beats.narration_text: Sofia should have asked Claude to flag its assumptions, not fix its reasoning. 'List any inference steps where you assumed something rather than derived it from the provided information.' That output is a checklist for a human to inspect — not a repair.

This applies to any reasoning task. Ask Claude to build an argument, then ask it to check the argument — and the errors that slip past the first pass will slip past the second. The check gives you false confidence in an output that still contains the original flaw.

An investment analyst asks Claude to build a DCF model rationale and then check the rationale for flaws. Claude returns: checked — no logical errors found. The analyst submits it. A senior partner points out that the rationale implicitly assumes a constant discount rate — which the model never questioned because it did not know to question it.

The analyst's fix: ask Claude to list every assumption in the rationale explicitly — including implicit ones. Claude lists seven. The constant discount rate is assumption four. The analyst reads it and flags it before submitting. The human catches what the sieve cannot.

The practice: never ask Claude to fix its own reasoning errors. Instead ask it to make its reasoning visible — list the assumptions, flag the inference steps, mark what it derived versus what it assumed. Then you review those, not another generated output.
The sieve that generates the error also reviews it. Ask Claude to expose its reasoning — not to verify it. Then apply your judgment where the model's cannot reach.

card.copy: The sieve that generates also reviews.
card.sub: Expose the reasoning. Apply your judgment.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why AI Can't Fix Its Own Mistakes] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
